stem: A community center asked everyone who attended its Tuesday evening yoga class to rate their satisfaction with the facility on a scale of 1 to 10. The average rating was 9.2. The director concluded that members of the community, in general, are highly satisfied with the facility. Which of the following best explains why this conclusion may not be valid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The rating scale should have included more options than 1 to 10.

choices.label: B
choices.text: People who attend yoga class are not a random sample of all community members.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The average rating of 9.2 is too high to be a reliable measure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The survey should have been given on multiple days to get a larger sample.

explanation: The director surveyed only people who attended Tuesday yoga class — a specific, self-selected group that already uses and presumably enjoys the facility. This group is not a random sample of the broader community, so their opinions cannot reliably be generalized to all community members. The conclusion is flawed because the sampling method introduces bias: people who come to yoga class are likely more satisfied than the average community member.

distractorRationale.A: Misidentifying the population — a student might focus on the measurement tool (the scale) rather than who was measured, incorrectly thinking the problem lies in the rating instrument rather than the sample selection.
distractorRationale.C: Wrong confidence interpretation — a student might mistakenly believe that an average this extreme (9.2 out of 10) is inherently suspicious as a statistical artifact, rather than recognizing the real issue is who was sampled.
distractorRationale.D: Sample size misapplication — a student might correctly notice the sample is limited but draw the wrong lesson: thinking more days of the same biased survey would fix the problem, when the flaw is the type of people surveyed, not the number of survey days.
